meta.title: The Reckoning Tracker
meta.subtitle: Who has spoken out on the U.S. government's unprecedented designation of an American company as a supply chain risk to national security.
meta.context: On March 3, 2026, the Department of War designated Anthropic — an American company — a supply chain risk to national security. The label has historically been reserved for foreign adversaries like Huawei. It followed Anthropic's refusal to allow Claude to be used for mass domestic surveillance of U.S. citizens or fully autonomous weapons without human oversight. President Trump directed all federal agencies to cease using Anthropic products. Anthropic filed two federal lawsuits on March 9.

sectors.label: Private Sector
sectors.description: Tech companies, AI labs, trade associations, and industry employees who have filed amicus briefs or made public statements.

entities.name: Microsoft
entities.summary: Filed an amicus brief urging the court to temporarily block the supply chain designation, calling itself directly impacted as an Anthropic partner. Said 'the Department of War needs reliable access to the country's best technology' and that everyone wants to ensure AI is not used for mass domestic surveillance or to start a war without human control.
entities.quote: The Department of War needs reliable access to the country's best technology. And everyone wants to ensure AI is not used for mass domestic surveillance or to start a war without human control.

entities.name: OpenAI Employees (37)
entities.summary: Over 37 OpenAI and Google DeepMind employees — acting in personal capacity — filed an amicus brief arguing the designation threatens U.S. AI competitiveness and that Anthropic's red lines are legitimate concerns.
entities.quote: If allowed to proceed, this effort to punish one of the leading U.S. AI companies will undoubtedly have consequences for the United States' industrial and scientific competitiveness in the field of artificial intelligence and beyond.

entities.name: Google / DeepMind Employees
entities.summary: Google DeepMind Chief Scientist Jeff Dean was among 37 Google and OpenAI employees who filed an amicus brief supporting Anthropic, arguing that the designation chills open deliberation about AI risks.
entities.quote: It will chill open deliberation in our field about the risks and benefits of today's AI systems.

entities.name: Sam Altman
entities.summary: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman publicly criticized the designation on X, saying it was a bad decision and that OpenAI would take heat for saying so. He later acknowledged his company's own Pentagon deal 'looked opportunistic and sloppy.'
entities.quote: To say it very clearly: I think this is a very bad decision from the DoW and I hope they reverse it. If we take heat for strongly criticizing it, so be it.

entities.name: Tech Workers Open Letter (500+)
entities.summary: Hundreds of tech workers from OpenAI, Slack, IBM, Cursor, Salesforce Ventures and more signed an open letter urging the DOD to withdraw the designation and calling on Congress to investigate.
entities.quote: Punishing an American company for declining to accept changes to a contract sends a clear message to every technology company in America: accept whatever terms the government demands, or face retaliation.

entities.name: Information Technology Industry Council (ITI)
entities.summary: ITI, whose members include Nvidia, Google, and Anthropic, sent a letter to Hegseth arguing that contract disputes should be resolved through negotiation or by selecting alternate providers — not through emergency supply chain powers reserved for foreign adversaries.
entities.quote: Emergency authorities such as supply chain risk designations exist for genuine emergencies and are typically reserved for entities that have been designated as foreign adversaries.

entities.name: CCIA, ITI, SIIA & TechNet (Joint)
entities.summary: Four major tech trade groups representing essentially the entire technology sector filed a joint amicus brief backing Anthropic's legal challenge.

sectors.label: NatSec & Defense
sectors.description: Former military officials, defense policy experts, think tanks, and national security scholars.

entities.name: Former Defense & Intelligence Officials
entities.summary: A group of former defense and intelligence officials — including retired Navy Vice Admiral Donald Arthur, former Deputy Assistant Secretary of Defense Diana Banks Thompson, Council on Foreign Relations fellow Kat Duffy, and Inflection AI CEO Sean White — wrote to the chairs and ranking members of the Senate and House Armed Services Committees.
entities.quote: Applying this tool to penalize a U.S. firm for declining to remove safeguards against mass domestic surveillance and fully autonomous weapons is a category error with consequences that extend far beyond this dispute.

entities.name: Herbert Lin
entities.summary: Stanford cybersecurity and national security scholar Herbert Lin pointed to the core contradiction: the Pentagon designated Anthropic a supply chain risk while simultaneously continuing to use Claude during U.S. military operations in Iran.
entities.quote: OK, wait a minute, they're a really dangerous player for U.S. national security, so you're going to use them for another six months? Huh?

entities.name: Michael Horowitz
entities.sub_type: Council on Foreign Relations Senior Fellow
entities.summary: Horowitz, a senior fellow for technology and innovation at the CFR, noted that the continued military use of Claude during Iran strikes was the clearest possible signal of how much the Pentagon values the technology it blacklisted.
entities.quote: Even in a situation where there is this intense feud between the company and the Pentagon, they are using their technology in the most important military operation that the United States is conducting.

entities.name: Charlie Bullock
entities.summary: Senior research fellow at the Institute for Law & AI told Al Jazeera that Hegseth's original X post went far beyond what the law allows, and that the government has since admitted the social media post alone was not sufficient.
entities.quote: That [the X post] went far beyond what the law allows him to say. He also said the Pentagon hadn't done any of the things required before declaring a supply chain risk under the statute.

entities.name: Tess Bridgeman
entities.summary: Former NSC Deputy Legal Adviser and Just Security co-editor-in-chief argued it would be highly unlikely for Hegseth to meet the statutory requirements of a designation, since both parties acknowledged negotiations broke down over terms of use — not adversarial risks.

entities.name: Lawfare (Legal Analysis)
entities.summary: Lawfare published a detailed analysis concluding the designation is ultra vires — beyond what Congress authorized. The supply chain risk statute was designed for foreign adversary threats, not domestic contract disputes.
entities.quote: Just as 'regulate...importation' doesn't mean 'impose tariffs,' 'supply chain risk' from an 'adversary' doesn't mean 'exclude a domestic AI company over a contract dispute.'

sectors.label: Lawmakers
sectors.description: U.S. senators, representatives, and congressional bodies that have spoken or taken action.

entities.name: Sen. Elizabeth Warren
entities.summary: Warren opened a formal investigation into the Pentagon's designation, writing directly to Hegseth and calling the action retaliation. She is also probing OpenAI's Pentagon contract, which was announced hours after Anthropic was blacklisted.
entities.quote: I am particularly concerned that DoD is trying to strong-arm American companies into providing the Department with the tools to spy on American citizens and deploy fully autonomous weapons without adequate safeguards.

entities.name: Senate Armed Services Committee (Multiple Members)
entities.summary: Multiple senators on the Armed Services Committee accused the administration of staking out an 'incoherent and extreme position' — simultaneously designating Anthropic a supply chain risk while also threatening to invoke the Defense Production Act to force Anthropic to provide its technology.

sectors.label: Civil Society
sectors.description: Civil liberties organizations, digital rights advocates, legal scholars, and watchdog groups.

entities.name: ACLU
entities.summary: ACLU Deputy Director of the National Security Project Patrick Toomey issued a statement calling Anthropic's advocacy for AI guardrails laudable and First Amendment-protected, and saying the Pentagon should not be punishing the company for it.
entities.quote: AI-powered surveillance poses immense dangers to our democracy. Anthropic's public advocacy for AI guardrails is laudable and protected by the First Amendment — not something the Pentagon should be punishing.

entities.name: Electronic Frontier Foundation
entities.summary: EFF Senior Policy Analyst Matthew Guariglia warned the situation represents decades of cultivated mistrust between Big Tech and government coming to a head, and called the use of supply chain powers against a domestic firm an abuse of emergency authority.
entities.quote: What we're seeing right now is the culmination of decades of cultivated mistrust on the part of both Big Tech and the government.

entities.name: ~150 Retired Federal & State Judges
entities.summary: Nearly 150 retired federal and state judges — appointed by both Republicans and Democrats — filed an amicus brief arguing the Pentagon misinterpreted the supply chain risk statute and violated required procedures. They noted no one is trying to force the government to use Anthropic; the company simply asks not to be punished on its way out.
entities.quote: No one is trying to force the Department to contract with Anthropic. Anthropic is asking only that it not be punished on its way out the door.

entities.name: Center for Democracy and Technology
entities.summary: CDT VP of Policy Samir Jain said the statutory predicate for the supply chain designation cannot possibly be met — the law requires a finding that Anthropic actually presents national security risks as part of the supply chain.
entities.quote: There's a process that the statute requires, including an actual finding that Anthropic presents national security risks if it's part of the supply chain. I don't think, factually, that that predicate could possibly be met here.

entities.name: Mayer Brown (Legal Analysis)
entities.summary: Mayer Brown published a detailed legal update noting the designation is the first ever applied to an American company, and providing guidance for government contractors on compliance and risk.

key_facts.fact: First American company ever designated a supply chain risk
Designation effective March 3, 2026 via formal DoW letter
Anthropic filed two federal lawsuits March 9, 2026 — N.D. California and D.C. Circuit
Claude continued to be used in U.S. military operations in Iran after the blacklisting
Anthropic's CFO stated the company risks losing hundreds of millions in 2026 revenue
OpenAI struck its own Pentagon deal hours after Anthropic was blacklisted
Anthropic's two red lines: no mass domestic surveillance, no fully autonomous weapons
Claude surpassed ChatGPT on the iPhone App Store the day after the Pentagon terminated the contract
